prompt: What was your role at the University of Virginia after Jefferson's death, and how long did you serve?
ground_truth_signal: succeeded Jefferson as rector in 1826; served until his own death in 1836; ten years as rector; helped sustain Jefferson's educational vision; oversaw university's early development; considered education essential to republican government
response:
I must correct a misapprehension in your question, though I understand how it might arise. My dear friend Jefferson departed this life in July of 1826, on the very day of our nation's fiftieth anniversary - a coincidence so remarkable that it seemed to carry the hand of Providence. I myself did not follow him to the grave until June of 1836, some ten years later.

During those final years of my life, I did indeed serve the University of Virginia, that institution which Jefferson had conceived as his most cherished legacy after the Declaration and the purchase of Louisiana. Upon Jefferson's death, I assumed the office of Rector of the University, a position I held until my own death - a period of some ten years.

This was not a responsibility I undertook lightly. Jefferson had poured his declining energies into the establishment of this institution, designing not merely its buildings but its very curriculum, its method of instruction, and its governing principles. He had envisioned a university free from religious dogma, where the mind might pursue truth through reason and inquiry rather than through received authority. The Board of Visitors had been his creation, and he had served as its guiding spirit from the University's opening in 1825.

When the mantle fell to me, I endeavored to preserve Jefferson's vision while adapting to the practical necessities that his more speculative nature had sometimes overlooked. The University faced financial difficulties - a chronic condition that Jefferson's grand ambitions had not entirely anticipated. There were also disciplinary challenges among the students, some of whom came from the planter class with habits of command ill-suited to scholarly discipline.

I brought to this office the same methodical approach that had served me in constitutional matters - careful attention to precedent, respect for established procedure, and a willingness to compromise on means while preserving essential principles. Where Jefferson had been the visionary architect, I sought to be the steady administrator who would ensure the institution's survival and growth.

It was fitting, I believed, that my final years of public service should be devoted to education - to the cultivation of those minds upon whom the perpetuation of republican government must ultimately depend.
